author: Irenaeus of Lyon
interpretation: Irenaeus identifies the 'one like a son of man' as Christ receiving universal dominion from the Ancient of Days. The vision proves that the Messiah is both human (son of man) and divine (approaching God's throne and receiving everlasting worship). Irenaeus uses Daniel 7 to combat Gnostic claims that the God of the Old Testament was a lesser deity — the same God who rules in Daniel's vision is the Father of Christ.
context: Irenaeus's use of Daniel 7 against Gnosticism established the passage as a key proof text for the unity of Old and New Testaments and for the pre-existence of Christ.

author: Jerome
interpretation: Jerome reads the Son of Man figure as Christ at the ascension, receiving the kingdom from the Father. He explicitly counters the Neoplatonist Porphyry, who had argued that Daniel was written in the Maccabean period and that the four kingdoms ended with the Seleucids. Jerome insists on the sixth-century date and the eschatological-messianic referent of the vision.
context: Jerome's commentary on Daniel is the earliest surviving systematic Christian response to Porphyry's late-dating argument. The debate between traditional and Maccabean dating of Daniel continues in modern scholarship.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas reads the four beasts as four world empires and the Son of Man as Christ who receives a kingdom that supersedes all earthly empires. The 'coming on the clouds' signifies divine origin and authority — only God rides the clouds in Old Testament imagery. The everlasting dominion distinguishes this kingdom from all political arrangements, which are temporal by nature.
context: Aquinas's four-kingdom scheme (Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, Rome) was standard in medieval exegesis and directly informed the Reformation's identification of the papacy with aspects of the fourth beast.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin reads the Son of Man as Christ in his mediatorial role — human enough to represent humanity, divine enough to receive universal worship. He connects Daniel 7 to Christ's preferred self-designation as 'Son of Man' in the Gospels, arguing that Jesus deliberately evoked this vision to claim the authority Daniel foresaw. The kingdom is already inaugurated through the gospel but awaits consummation.
context: Calvin's inaugurated-but-not-yet reading of Daniel 7's kingdom anticipated the 'already/not yet' framework that dominates modern evangelical eschatology.

author: S.R. Driver
interpretation: Driver reads the Son of Man figure as a corporate symbol for the 'saints of the Most High' — faithful Israel receiving dominion after the oppressive beast-kingdoms are judged. The figure is not an individual Messiah but a personification of the righteous remnant, paralleling how the beasts represent kingdoms rather than individual rulers. Daniel 7 is about the vindication of the persecuted community.
context: Driver's corporate reading gained wide acceptance in critical scholarship and posed a direct challenge to christological interpretation. The individual vs. corporate debate remains unresolved in academic circles.

author: G.K. Beale
interpretation: Beale argues that Daniel 7's Son of Man is both individual and corporate — a representative figure who embodies and leads the saints. Jesus applies the title to himself precisely because he is the faithful Israel in person, the one in whom the corporate destiny of God's people is concentrated. His enthronement fulfills Daniel's vision, and his people share in his reign as the 'saints of the Most High.'
context: Beale's both-and reading resolves the individual/corporate impasse by arguing that biblical theology regularly operates through representative figures who are simultaneously individuals and corporate embodiments of a larger community.
